speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Renee Firestone on site in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it-- it's really hard for me to recreate exactly what this place was. This-- this must have been the oven when-- where they were heating those barrels, where the steam was coming from. I don't know. But every nook-- every, every nook here-- every corner is so scary. It's-- there is death around here everywhere. That's all you feel. You just feel death present.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and-- and you keep asking yourself, how many died here? How many prisoners worked at this oven, and how many of them were destroyed afterwards? I- I just-- I just can't describe how I feel. I can't describe how I-- I'm trying to understand-- make some sense of this, but it just doesn't make any sense. No matter-- no matter how hard you try, it just doesn't make any sense. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: These are these barrels where the clothing was disinfected. I told you. The clothes that were-- that came off dead bodies, especially. Or if you changed from one camp to another, you came into the bath house again. They re-shaved your head, disinfected you and your clothing. I remember just before we left Auschwitz we stood out there naked, waiting for our clothes to be finished so we can get on our death march. But just, as I said before, just every corner here, [SIGHS] you can sense-- you can sense death. You can sense destruction, evil-- just an evil place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is where some of the people were unloaded. When the crematorias were overloaded and people had to wait to be gassed. They came to that road, and they were-- they were spread around here. Women, old women, children were sitting around waiting to decide what's going to happen to them. And many times, when the crematorias were, as I said, overloaded-- and during the final solution, it was almost all the time-- then they-- then they dug these pits. There is one over there also, I see. And I think that's the children's pit. I'm not sure. And they were burning people in these pits. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] These pits were prepared in such a way that it drained the-- the fat from the people that were burning, and it collected the fat in a place. And then they-- they doused the new people with that fat, so that the pit should be burning well through the whole time. You know, when-- when I see these things now, and-- and-- and I try to remember these things, only then can I understand how devilish this whole place was-- how premeditated, preplanned-- every-- every corner, every step that the prisoners made was-- was preplanned. They knew exactly what's going to happen to us. It's unbelievable. It's unbelievable, and we were so naive or stupid that we just walked right into it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course, what human mind could have imagined what was going on here? No normal human mind can understand it or imagine it. I still cannot-- still cannot believe, still cannot believe what I have seen here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And look at it. It's so peaceful, so quiet. The other day there were two deer roaming around this place. There was nothing alive here-- nothing. Everything was dead. Even those of us who stayed in the camps were already dead. And we knew-- we knew that we are dead. We knew that there is no way we are going to survive. It is really only by a miracle that-- that it happened-- that-- that I'm here only by the grace of God. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] You know, it's so funny. I-- I've been talking to children in schools about my experiences for 20 years. Now that I'm standing here, the words just don't come, because there are no words for this. [SOB] Whatever I- I- I say is so meaningless, so trivial. You can't-- you can't really describe this. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SIGHS] Forgive me. I just-- there's just nothing I can say about this place. [SIGHS] I understand that some Russian soldiers, prisoners of war are buried here-- Jews and non-Jews-- and that's what represents the crosses and-- and the Stars of David. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] [SIGHS] [SOB] I-- I see these guard houses. We were watched from those towers every moment-- every move we made. We couldn't speak to each other. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Every human being who-- who survived here was on his own. And every life was in danger every moment of the stay here. Nobody believed that we ever will leave this place. We just-- we just couldn't believe it because we saw what was going on here. [SIGHS] The chimneys were burning 24 hours a day, and the fire was bellowing out of it. You could see it. You could smell it. It permeated this whole territory. You know, people in the city say that they didn't know what was going on. It's impossible. It's impossible. The smell permeated this place in such a way that it ate itself-- the smell ate itself into our flesh. For years, I could smell it on myself. There's just no way they didn't know. No way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what should we say about those who claim that it never happened? How can we convince people? Look around. How can we convince people that there was a-- a place that resembled only Hell? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I hope that the world will see it while it is still here, because we are not going to be here much longer. And once we, the survivors, are gone, I'm afraid the world will completely forget. I hope-- I-- I hope not, but unfortunately, that's what I feel.

speaker: interviewer
text: It is-- half of it is already destroyed. How much longer that the whole place is going to be cleared? And who knows? Some apartment houses will be built over this place where we left our loved ones-- where there are no graves for them. [SIGHS] There's a little river, not far from here, called the Vistula, and the ashes were disposed in that river. And I understand that, even today, at summertime when the river is flowing, you can see bones and teeth on the surface. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] How can we tell these people who say it never happened? Come here and look around. See what's going on. See-- see what it looks like. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] You see, when you look around, you see nothing here. But when I look around, I see the thousands of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] frightened human beings sitting, waiting. [SOB] [SIGHS] [SIGHS] Please don't ever forget. I'm asking you. You are still young. You'll be here for awhile. Please, don't let the world forget. [SOB] [PAUSES FOR 21 SECONDS] Let's go. [SOB] [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's the plaque in memory of Edith Stein. She was a nun who was brought here because she was Jewish. She-- she had a Jewish grandparent or maybe even a parent. I'm not sure. But some part of her was Jewish. So she was brought to Auschwitz as a Jew and killed here. And that's-- that's the plaque that, probably, the Carmelite nuns put up for her. I-- I think that she became a saint. I'm not sure. There was a-- I remember a controversy about whether she should or should not be made into a saint but I don't know what eventually happened. But that's her name-- Edith Stein.
